title: Temporality
medium: Screen, projection, camera, charcoal on paper, digital renders, video
short: Triptychs that present different elements of time, and the relationship between viewer and artist.
long:
Temporality is a fickle concept. Each hour, minute, second, passes constantly, everchanging and ever evolving. Humanity has always been in a quest to capture time. To pause, to warp, to displace, the constant flow. We make paintings, drawings, pictures, videos, in a constant effort to capture time. In the information age, we are suspended in an anachronic experience. The camera phone has forever changed our interaction with the world. To capture moments at our will and collect them in our pockets. We present these images to the world and consume the experiences of others. In an effort to capture the essence of temporality, I have attempted to present ‘time’ as a metaphysical transmedia installation, that places the viewer between triptychs, in works that present different elements of time, and the relationship between viewer and artist. 

The first is the abstract. The drawing itself is a physical consequence of the act of drawing. The video is the displacement of the drawing outside time. The drawing itself is a production of an act, produced in a timeframe. The lines, the gestures, and the imprints are physical measurements of time. The physical drawing is masked behind the projections of a video of my drawing process, as well as mixed media animations, layered over each other until none of the images are decipherable anymore.The video, in its abstraction, takes the work away from the physical. It is no longer an act that leads to a final production, but instead the act of drawing as a whole. 

The second is the liminal. A series of video renders on the screens present in the space loop indefinitely. Instead of being a single, atemporal video, they are an act with a start and a finish. This is time measured, time with a beginning and an end. Using 3d models I made of myself, I created physics-based simulations where I throw myself across a digital room, with virtual scans of elements from my apartment. The screens are placed across the room, encouraging the viewer to move around the space, and create dynamism to the space.

The third is the physical. As the viewer explores the space and the different elements, they are confronted by the realization of being within a physical time and space. A constant video feed of the installation is projected in the space, and the viewer can see themselves in real time experiencing the space. In the final segment, the viewers insert themselves into the artwork, their physical image now present alongside my works.
